text: uh imagine a king okay but you know do not imagine him sitting on the throne do not imagine him
wearing a big gold crown or uh or holding a royal staff right take all that away yeah strip all that
away instead uh i want you to imagine a hero a man who you know killed giants when he was young
a man who brought a totally broken fractured country together a very powerful figure exactly
but now picture this man old his hair is gray he is walking up a rough dirt hill and um he is
crying yeah his head is completely covered with a cloth because he is just so deeply ashamed
and perhaps the most shocking part of all this he has no shoes on his feet it is um it is a very
jarring image i mean it completely deconstructs it takes apart our whole idea of what a king should
look like it really does yeah because he's running away from his own home he's fleeing his own
uh the person chasing him the person organizing a whole army to kill him yeah it is his own son
it is a total tragedy it is like a play by shakespeare but you know it was written thousands
of years before shakespeare was even born yeah it is probably one of the saddest pictures in
the entire source text it really is so welcome back to the deep dive we are so glad you are
here with us today we have a very very specific focus yes you do we are looking at this incredible
of betrayal of deep sadness but also of just incredible loyalty we are doing a deep dive
into second samuel chapter 15 specifically just chapter 15 right we are going to really zoom in
we want to focus very closely on just this one chapter we are going to put blinders onto the
rest of the book and just see how this specific piece of the story is built and we have a special
mission for today's deep dive we are not just going to read the story to uh find out what
happened no we want to look deeper yeah we are going to use a special mission to read the story
to understand it better we are looking at the source material from the scholar robert alter
he wrote a very famous book called the art of biblical narrative that is right and robert alter
absalom king david's son chapter 15 starts with absalom making a very big very public move he
really does and it is a very visual move the text tells us absalom got for himself a carriage and
horses and 50 runners to go before him 50 runners yeah 50 men running ahead of his carriage i mean
that seems incredibly excessive he is just a prince right why on earth does he need 50 men
running in front of him well you have to think about the visual image here in ancient times
especially in the rocky hills where jerusalem is located people usually rode mules or donkeys
right very simple animals exactly even the king rode a mule it was considered a humble steady
but a chariot a big carriage pulled by horses that was a war machine like a piece of military
equipment yes it was basically the ancient version of a tank oh wow so if you see someone driving a
tank down the main street and they have 50 security guards in matching uniforms running
in front of them you pay attention you definitely pay attention oh absalom is putting on a massive
show he is acting exactly like a king before he is actually the king he is loudly telling the
people look at me i am powerful i am strong
i am the future of this country he is basically building his brand he is making himself look
incredibly important to the average person on the street that is a great way to put it he is building
a brand based on power and modern strength but the thing is he does not just rely on the cool
chariot no he also uses a very specific very clever psychological technique to get the regular
people to like him right the text describes what we can call the man at the gate technique can you
tell us about how that works sure so in ancient walled cities the city gate was not just a heavy
door to keep enemies out it was the center of public life like a town square exactly it was
like the courthouse and the city hall combined if you had a legal problem maybe your neighbor stole
your sheep or you had a big disagreement about who owned a certain field you went to the city
gate to find a judge to decide who was right and the story says absalom goes there very early in
the morning he just stands right by the road leading into the gate yes and this is exactly

where robert alters idea about the idea of the man at the gate and the story says absalom goes there very early in the morning he just stands right by the road leading into the gate yes and this is exactly where robert alters idea about the
importance of dialogue becomes so clear we learn about absalom's true character not just by what
the narrator tells us about him but by what absalom actually says out loud to the people
we have to pay attention to his exact words we really do i have the text right here
so when someone comes up the road with a legal problem absalom stops them and asks
where they are some he acts super friendly and then he says this line he says see
your cause is good and right but there is no one from the king to hear you
right there that is such a brilliant and totally dangerous sentence why is it dangerous i mean on
the surface it sounds really nice he is validating them look closely at the two separate things he
does in that one sentence first he flatters the person he says your cause is good but he does not
even hear the other side of the story he does not look at any evidence or call any witnesses he just
immediately tells the person you are right you win and as we know everyone loves to hear that they
are right that is very true if i go to a judge with the problem and the judge tells me i am
right before the trial even starts i am gonna really like that judge exactly it creates this
instant powerful connection but then look at the second half of his sentence he says
but there is no one from the king to hear you oh wow he is planting a seed of doubt yes
he is essentially saying the system is broken my father king david does not care about your
problems he is too busy he is getting too old he has not set up a proper
system to actually help you get justice so he is completely blaming the current administration
he is telling the regular people that the government is failing them yes and then
naturally once he has created this feeling of anger and frustration he offers himself as the
only solution he says oh that i were made judge in the land then every man who has any suit or
cause might come to me and i would do him justice if i were in charge i would fix it all exactly that
is just a classic politician's speech it is timeless we see this exact
same strategy in politics today and we are reading about it happening
three thousand years ago he creates the problem by saying the king is too busy
and then he presents himself as the wonderful savior who will listen
and he goes out of his way to not act like a distant snobby royal does he not at all the text
says when people try to bow down to him to show respect he would stop them he would reach out grab
their hand and actually kiss them this is the common man touch in those days you were absolutely
bowed to a prince it was the law but absalom says no no do not bow to me we are equals i am your
friend he is breaking down that big barrier between the royal family and the normal citizens
he is making every single person he meets feel incredibly special yes and the text uses a very
famous very specific phrase here to describe the final result of all this hard work at the gate i
know the one you mean it says so absalom stole the hearts of the men of israel now that phrase
in english that sounds very romantic and poetic like a love song but what does it actually mean
in the original hebrew language that is a really great question because it is not about romance at
all in ancient hebrew thinking the heart the word is lev is not just about your feelings or
your emotions the heart is the center of your will it is the center of your mind your logic
and your decision making it is where you make your choices oh i see so he did not just make the
heart he literally stole their loyalty he stole their vote precisely to steal a heart means he
took something that rightfully did not belong to him their loyalty legally and morally belonged
to the king to david but absalom tricked them into giving it to him instead so it is theft
yes he did not earn their loyalty through years of hard work or brave service to the country
he just took it through charm cheap tricks and really smart manipulation okay so this
slow manipulation goes on for a long time the text says 40 years though some historical manuscripts
but either way it is a long period of waiting it is a long game right absalom is standing at the
gate stealing hearts slowly building his secret army of supporters and then everything shifts
which brings us to part two of our story the conspiracy yes the plot moves out into the open
and this takes place at a city called hebron now geography is super important here we really need
to understand the map to get why this matters okay lay it out for us what is hebron hebron is the old
capital city it is where david first became king many years ago before he ever conquered jerusalem
so it is a very historic very holy place for the people that makes sense and absalom goes to his
father king david and asks for official permission to travel there but he obviously does not say hey
dad i am going to hebron to start a massive rebellion and take your throne no he tells a lie
and it is a very specific religious lie he says please let me go and pay my vow
which i have vowed to the lord a vow is like a very serious promise to god or
exactly he tells his father that he made a sacred promise to god while he was living far away from
home in a place called gishor and now he needs to go to the holy city of hebron to worship and
fulfill that promise that is pretty twisted honestly he is using religious worship as a
total cover-up for high treason it really shows us the dark character of absalom
he is perfectly willing to use god's name and his father's respect for god
to hide his plot to kill his own father it is incredibly cynical
now here is where i want to use that tool from robert alter that we talked about earlier
the tool of silence yes this is a perfect place for it because i want to look at david's response
to this lie absalom asks to go and david just says two words he says go in peace
and that is it this is a textbook example of narrative reticence reticence right that is
just a fancy academic word meaning the writer is intentionally holding back information from
the reader the text absolutely does not tell us what david is thinking in this moment
is just blank right is david suspicious does he notice the 50 runners and the chariot
does he know absalom is lying to his face it honestly feels like he does not know anything
that is the effect of the silence it suggests that david is completely in the dark or perhaps he is
just in deep denial you have to remember absalom is the son he deeply loves right a father's blind
spot exactly the writer leaves it blank so we can really feel the tragedy of david's ignorance here
